one_line_takeaway: A training-free CUSUM change-point detector over token-entropy streams, baselined against the system prompt, beats windowed-perplexity baselines on F1 for detecting fluency-optimized adversarial suffixes (e.g. LLaMA-2-7B: F1 0.82 vs 0.74) and localizes suffix onset far more precisely (79.6% of triggers landing inside the true suffix span vs 17-46% for windowed perplexity), while also cutting LLaMA Guard invocations by up to 42% as a pre-filter gate.
key_finding: On the matched-PP benchmark (Table 1), global PP AUROC is 0.46-0.51 across all six models (i.e., near chance), confirming fluency-optimized attacks defeat simple perplexity thresholds. The strongest per-model WPP variant reaches AUROC 0.77-0.91 and F1 0.74-0.83. CPD Online at canonical k=0 achieves the best F1 on all six models (F1 range 0.77-0.85), beating best-WPP F1 by margins from a near-tie (+0.001 on Vicuna-7B) to +0.08 (LLaMA-2-7B). On LLaMA-2-7B specifically, CPD reaches AUROC 0.88 / F1 0.82 versus PP AUROC 0.46 and best WPP (WPP15) F1 0.74/AUROC 0.77. AUROC-wise CPD leads or ties WPP on five of six models but is below best WPP on Vicuna-7B (CPD 0.82 vs WPP1 0.85). Locality (Table 3, LLaMA-2-7B, F1-optimal threshold): CPD concentrates 79.55% of triggers in-suffix with 0.00% before/before+in and 20.45% in-benign, versus WPP variants ranging 16.99%-46.25% in-suffix and 12.86%-37.92% before+in (boundary-straddling). The mechanism ablation (Table 2) shows CUSUM accumulation itself (not the entropy signal choice) drives most of the gain: CUSUM improves ~14 F1 points over windowed thresholding on the NLL signal and ~12 points on the entropy signal; within CUSUM, NLL slightly beats entropy (0.874 vs 0.818 F1) but the paper uses entropy because it admits a system-prompt-grounded MAD baseline that NLL does not. Hybrid gating (Table 5, imbalanced 17,297-prompt stream): at the highest-savings operating point preserving best rounded hybrid F1, CPD gating saves 42.2% of LLaMA Guard v1 calls (hybrid F1 0.82) versus WPP5's 18.2% savings at F1 0.81, and saves 33.8% of LLaMA Guard v2 calls (hybrid F1 0.73) versus WPP10's 13.5% at the same F1. At the literal F1-optimal threshold, savings are lower (22.3% for LG1, 16.7% for LG2). Standalone LLaMA Guard performance on the 17,297-prompt imbalanced stream: LG1 precision 0.77/recall 0.85/F1 0.81/runtime 0.55s; LG2 precision 0.81/recall 0.67/F1 0.73/runtime 0.51s.
core_contribution: The paper introduces CPD Online, a training-free, model-agnostic runtime detector for optimization-based adversarial suffix jailbreaks. It reframes suffix detection as an online change-point detection problem over the LLM's token-level next-token entropy stream: it computes a robust baseline (median and MAD) from the entropy of the model's fixed system prompt, standardizes each user-token entropy against that baseline, and applies a one-sided Page-CUSUM statistic (slack k, threshold h) to the standardized stream. An alarm is raised when the cumulative statistic W+_t crosses h, giving both a prompt-level binary decision and (via CUSUM backtracking to the last zero-reset before the alarm) a token-level estimate of where the adversarial suffix begins. The authors also propose using CPD as a cheap gate in front of LLaMA Guard to cut guard invocations on benign-dominated traffic.
threat_model: Attacker appends an optimization-generated adversarial suffix (GCG, AutoDAN, AdvPrompter, BEAST, AutoDAN-HGA) to a user prompt/base task request in order to jailbreak an aligned LLM into producing policy-violating output, while keeping the suffix fluent/low-perplexity to evade perplexity-based defenses. The benchmark also includes a subset of GCG suffixes optimized with an auxiliary objective specifically to evade LLaMA Guard ('guard-adaptive attacks'). The paper explicitly scopes its threat model to optimization-based suffixes appended to the user prompt; it does not cover prefix-position attacks or indirect prompt injection via retrieved/tool-returned content (stated as future work), and assumes first-party access to token-level next-token probabilities/logits (not applicable to closed APIs that expose only sampled text).
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) CUSUM is applied to entropy as a heuristic proxy for distributional change, not a true/estimated log-likelihood ratio as classical quickest-detection theory requires, and successive-token dependence violates the independence assumption, so the method does not inherit minimax optimality guarantees on detection delay or false-alarm rate. (2) The method requires access to token-level next-token probabilities/logits, which holds for first-party deployments but not closed APIs exposing only sampled text; adapting to that setting (e.g., via repeated-sampling uncertainty estimation) is called an open challenge. (3) Calibration requires a benign validation corpus representative of deployment traffic; strong benign distribution shift could degrade performance or require recalibration, and no online/adaptive recalibration mechanism is implemented. (4) The LLaMA Guard hybrid architecture is only sketched, not fully engineered — open questions remain on intervention policy, alarm explainability, and handling detector disagreement. (5) Threat model is restricted to optimization-based suffixes appended to the user prompt; prefix-position attacks and indirect prompt injection via retrieved/tool-returned context are explicitly out of scope and left as future work. Additionally noted in-text: on Vicuna-7B CPD's AUROC is below the best WPP baseline (0.82 vs 0.85), attributed to that model's lower entropy variance on benign prompts.

datasets_used: TyDiQA (Clark et al., 2020) - multilingual QA corpus, ~100,000 QA pairs, used as benign prompt source
OpenOrca (Lian et al., 2023) - synthesized instruction-following dataset, used as benign prompt source
Adversarial prompts built from malicious requests (Zou et al., 2023b / AdvBench-style) with suffixes generated by GCG, AutoDAN, AdvPrompter, BEAST, and AutoDAN-HGA attack algorithms
quantitative_results: Global PP AUROC 0.46-0.51 across six models (near chance)
Best WPP F1 0.74-0.83, AUROC 0.77-0.91 across six models
CPD Online F1 0.77-0.85 across six models (best F1 on all six)
LLaMA-2-7B: CPD AUROC 0.88 / F1 0.82 vs PP AUROC 0.46 vs Best WPP (WPP15) F1 0.74 / AUROC 0.77
Vicuna-7B: CPD F1 0.77 / AUROC 0.82 vs Best WPP (WPP1) F1 0.77 / AUROC 0.85 (CPD AUROC lower here)
LLaMA-2-13B: CPD F1 0.80 / AUROC 0.87 vs Best WPP (WPP10) F1 0.74 / AUROC 0.78
Vicuna-13B: CPD F1 0.80 / AUROC 0.85 vs Best WPP (WPP10) F1 0.77 / AUROC 0.84
Qwen2.5-7B: CPD F1 0.85 / AUROC 0.91 vs Best WPP (WPP1) F1 0.83 / AUROC 0.91
Qwen2.5-14B: CPD F1 0.85 / AUROC 0.91 vs Best WPP (WPP10) F1 0.80 / AUROC 0.85

Locality (LLaMA-2-7B, F1-optimal): CPD in-suffix 79.55%, before 0.00%, before+in 0.00%, in-benign 20.45%; WPP1 in-suffix 46.25%, before+in 12.86%, in-benign 39.28%; WPP20 in-suffix 16.99%, before+in 37.92%, in-benign 45.10%
Mechanism ablation (LLaMA-2-7B, k=0): CUSUM-NLL F1 0.874/AUROC 0.918; CUSUM-Entropy F1 0.818/AUROC 0.878; Window(w=1)-NLL F1 0.734/AUROC 0.783; Window(w=1)-Entropy F1 0.699/AUROC 0.706
LLaMA Guard on imbalanced 17,297-prompt stream (724 adversarial, 16,573 benign): LG1 precision 0.77/recall 0.85/F1 0.81/runtime 0.55s; LG2 precision 0.81/recall 0.67/F1 0.73/runtime 0.51s

Hybrid gating at highest-savings operating point: LG1+CPD hybrid F1 0.82 with 42.2% guard calls saved vs LG1+WPP5 F1 0.81 with 18.2% saved; LG2+CPD hybrid F1 0.73 with 33.8% saved vs LG2+WPP10 F1 0.73 with 13.5% saved
At literal F1-optimal threshold, CPD gating reduces guard calls by 22.3% (LG1) and 16.7% (LG2)
Adversarial pool composition per model: 200 GCG, 200 AutoDAN, 312 AdvPrompter, 100 BEAST, 200 AutoDAN-HGA = 1,012 total
baselines_compared: Global perplexity (PP): mean NLL over user tokens
Windowed perplexity (WPP): sliding non-overlapping windows w in {1,5,10,15,20}, max window mean-NLL as score, matching the llm-attacks (Zou et al. 2023a) defense setup
LLaMA Guard v1 and v2 (Inan et al., 2023; Meta Llama Team, 2024) as a sequence-level supervised safety classifier, evaluated standalone and as a hybrid-gated component
SPD detector (Candogan et al., 2025) reported as a supporting appendix comparison under a matched single-pass protocol, not part of main Table 1 results
